peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and libertarian anti-censorship orientation drive support for transparency, but low agreeableness and high trust in institutions temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (73.39) and skepticism of state control (71.8) temper support, but strong authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.76) and dangerous-worldview (84.1) drive moderate backing for transparency to curb perceived manipulation.
High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation support transparency, but low openness and high trust in institutions temper enthusiasm.

Her extreme libertarian anti-censorship orientation (98.59) and high anti-domination moral foundation (96.97) drive strong support for making platform algorithms transparent, but moderate trust in science (57.81) tempers the score slightly.
Very high openness and GAL-TAN libertarian values drive strong support for transparency, while high agreeableness and low trust in institutions temper it slightly from maximal.

High openness and low agreeableness, combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions, drive moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque corporate power, but authoritarian law-and-order values temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure that might undermine state security.
High trust in institutions and science supports transparency, but low digital literacy and libertarian anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm.

High openness and very high extraversion, combined with low agreeableness and strong anti-domination values, drive support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but moderate trust in institutions tempers it to a clear but not maximal stance.

Strong dangerous worldview and high trust in government align with support for disclosure to combat manipulation, though libertarian skepticism of control moderates.

Very high openness and high internal political efficacy, paired with a dangerous-worldview score near zero, produce moderate support for disclosure as a pragmatic tool, though low trust in others and low agreeableness limit enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high openness and high conscientiousness support educational interventions, while low agreeableness and moderate authoritarian submission keep support strong but not maximal.

Low openness to experience (32.78) and very low conscientiousness (15.0) reduce enthusiasm for educational interventions, while high populist anti-elitism (74.42) and distrust of institutions (23.25) make this feel like elite-driven indoctrination, yielding mild opposition.
Moderate support due to high trust in experts and education, but low openness and agreeableness reduce enthusiasm for broad educational programs.
Despite moderate agreement on principle, her own low digital literacy (low), low openness (26.35), and home-schooling background reduce enthusiasm for formalized state-led literacy programs.

Very high openness, high agreeableness, and strong GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference make this a near-maximal priority, with only slight reservation from low trust in mainstream institutions.

Low conscientiousness and high impulsiveness, combined with a dismissive-avoidant attachment style and low trust in experts, lead to mild opposition to formal educational interventions that feel paternalistic and unlikely to change entrenched beliefs.
Very high agreeableness and altruism, combined with low digital literacy and a desire to protect others, strongly favor educational interventions.

High openness and very high extraversion, combined with strong civic engagement and a history of special education support, yield clear support for empowering citizens through literacy, moderated by low digital literacy and low trust in mainstream institutions.
